vision.title: Forge Studio
vision.mission: Give teams a systematic way to build and use rich product and technical context in the IDE so AI-assisted delivery stays aligned from vision to shipped work—without replacing GitHub as the place where issue state lives.
vision.core_purpose: Engineers using Cursor or VS Code need shared, validated context in `.forge` and a clear view of what is still open in GitHub. Forge Studio bridges those worlds: agents and commands maintain intent and contracts locally, while delivery and backlog truth stay in GitHub. A dedicated in-IDE roadmap view makes incomplete work visible next to Forge context without copying issues into the repo.
vision.key_architecture: Product and structural intent live in `.forge` (vision, project anchor, knowledge map, domain contracts). Execution tracking—issues, milestones, and project fields—lives in GitHub. Forge reads from GitHub for roadmap and planning surfaces; it does not maintain a second backlog database in the repository.

strategic_goals.short_term: Ship a read-only Forge Roadmap experience: a command that opens a webview table of issues whose GitHub Projects v2 Status is not Done for the active Forge project, grouped by the Projects v2 Sprint field by default, with an in-view control to switch grouping to repository milestone. Use pass-through GitHub authentication (the IDE session); if the user is not signed into GitHub, prompt them to sign in before loading. In multi-root workspaces, prompt once for which workspace folder or active project to scope the view. Phase one is list-only—no edits or state changes from this UI. Defer dedicated rate-limit handling and client-side caching.
strategic_goals.medium_term: Deepen IDE integration between `.forge` intent and GitHub delivery: faster refresh, clearer empty and error states, and workflows that help Planner and engineers stay aligned without duplicating GitHub.
strategic_goals.long_term: Forge Studio remains the default context-engineering layer for Alto9 and community users: authoritative local intent, GitHub-authoritative delivery, and optional richer views and automation where they reduce friction without splitting truth.

key_principles.text: GitHub is the source of truth for issues, milestones, and project board fields. Forge surfaces that data; it does not imply a parallel backlog store in the repo.
Ship incrementally: prove value with read-only views and clear scope before adding mutations from the extension.
Project-scoped UX: roadmap and backlog views apply to one Forge-associated repository context at a time; multi-root workspaces must disambiguate which folder or project is active.

Roadmap semantics are fixed for v1: incomplete means the issue's GitHub Projects v2 Status is not Done; sprint grouping reads the Projects v2 Sprint field; milestone grouping uses the issue milestone. Data that requires Projects v2 uses the GitHub GraphQL API as needed.

Authentication is pass-through to the user's GitHub sign-in for the IDE. If not signed in, prompt to sign in before fetching roadmap data. Initial roadmap scope does not include rate-limit mitigation or client-side caching.

competitive_advantages.text: Forge combines structured agentic context (`.forge`) with live GitHub delivery state in the IDE, so teams plan and implement without tab churn or duplicate issue lists.
The staged Forge workflow (Product Owner through Quality Assurance) stays honest about ownership: local intent files vs. GitHub execution, with skills and agents aligned to that split.

differentiation_from_competitors.text: Unlike generic AI extensions that treat issues as an afterthought, Forge positions the backlog as GitHub-backed truth while `.forge` holds the why and how for agents.
Unlike standalone PM tools, Forge does not fork issue data into a proprietary store for the roadmap view—reducing drift and preserving GitHub as the workflow hub.
